_provenance: Claude-authored original Gaokao reading, batch D3. ~220-280 word passages, 4 MCQs each (detail/inference/main idea/attitude/vocab). Gaokao difficulty. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: In many parts of the world, clean water is a daily struggle. Millions of people, especially children, become ill or die each year from drinking dirty water. It was this problem that a young engineer named Daniel Okoro set out to solve. Growing up in a village where his family had to walk hours each day to fetch water, Okoro understood the problem from the inside. After studying engineering at university, he could have taken a comfortable, well-paid job in the city. Instead, he returned home, determined to find a cheap, simple way to make water safe. His goal was not to build something complex and expensive, but something ordinary people could afford and repair themselves. After years of trial and error, he developed a small, low-cost filter made largely from local materials. It used no electricity and was simple enough for anyone to use, and a single filter could provide clean water for an entire family for years. Okoro did not try to make himself rich from his invention. Instead, he shared the design freely, training local people to build and sell the filters themselves, so that the knowledge would spread and stay in the community. Today, his simple filters are used in thousands of homes. Okoro's story shows that the most valuable inventions are not always the most complicated. Sometimes, the greatest difference is made by a simple idea, created by someone who refuses to look away from a problem others have accepted.

questions.prompt: What problem did Okoro set out to solve?
What was special about his water filter?
options: It was cheap and simple, using local materials and no electricity.
It needed special power to run.

questions.prompt: What did Okoro do with his design?
options: He shared it freely and trained local people to build it.
He sold it to a big foreign company.

questions.prompt: What does Okoro's story show?
options: That the most valuable inventions are not always the most complicated.
That engineers should work only in cities.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 脏水致病)、细节(B 廉价简单、本地材料、无需电)、细节(B 免费分享设计、培训本地人)、主旨(B 最有价值的发明未必最复杂)。

sets.passage: When we think of pollution, we usually picture dirty air or rubbish-filled rivers. But there is another kind of pollution, less visible yet increasingly serious, that surrounds us every day: noise. Modern life is loud. Traffic, construction, machines and crowds fill our cities with a constant background of sound that few of us even notice any more. Yet research shows that this noise can do real harm. Beyond simply damaging our hearing, constant noise raises levels of stress, disturbs sleep, and has been linked to heart problems. Children who grow up near busy roads or airports, studies suggest, may even find it harder to learn and concentrate. The burden of noise is not shared equally. The poorest neighbourhoods are often the loudest, squeezed against motorways, railways and factories, while quieter areas remain a privilege of the wealthy. The good news is that noise pollution can be reduced, often at modest cost. Barriers along motorways, quieter road surfaces, lower speed limits and belts of trees can all soften the din. Yet noise is too often dismissed as a minor nuisance rather than treated as the health issue it is. Perhaps the first step is simply to start listening — to notice the noise we have learned to ignore, and to value quiet not as a luxury, but as a basic condition for a healthy life.

questions.prompt: What kind of pollution does the passage mainly discuss?
What harm can constant noise do?
options: It raises stress, disturbs sleep, and harms the heart and learning.

questions.prompt: Who tends to suffer most from noise?
What does the writer suggest?
options: That noise is only a minor nuisance.
That quiet should be valued as a basic condition for a healthy life.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 噪音污染)、细节(B 升压力、扰睡眠、伤心脏与学习)、细节(B 最穷社区最吵)、主旨/态度(B 应把安静当作健康的基本条件)。

sets.passage: In a small room full of computers, a group of volunteers spends its weekends performing a kind of quiet magic. They take old, damaged photographs — torn, faded, or water-stained — and carefully restore them to life. The project, run entirely by unpaid volunteers, repairs photos for families who have lost almost everything in floods, fires or war. For the families, these images are far more than pieces of paper. Often they are the only surviving picture of a lost parent, a childhood home, or a wedding day from long ago. When disaster destroys a home, such photographs may be the only thread connecting people to their past. The work is slow and demands great skill and patience. Using special software, a volunteer may spend many hours on a single image, rebuilding a missing face or a damaged corner, pixel by pixel. They work from old, blurry copies, doing their best to remain faithful to the original. What moves the volunteers most is the reaction of the families. Many weep when they see a restored photograph of a loved one they thought was lost forever. 'We are not really repairing photos,' one volunteer explains. 'We are giving people back their memories.' The project is a powerful reminder that, in a world that often measures worth in money, some of the most precious things we own cannot be bought at any price.

questions.prompt: What do the volunteers do?
Why are the photos so important to the families?
options: They are worth a lot of money.
They are often the only surviving link to a lost person or past.
They are very easy to copy.

questions.prompt: What is the restoration work like?
What does the project remind us?
options: That some of the most precious things cannot be bought at any price.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 修复老旧受损照片)、细节(B 常是与过去相连的唯一线索)、细节(B 缓慢、需技巧与耐心)、主旨(B 最珍贵之物无法用钱买到)。

sets.passage: The pangolin is one of the strangest and gentlest creatures on Earth. Covered in hard scales, it rolls into a tight ball when frightened, and it lives quietly on a diet of ants. Few people have heard of it. Yet the pangolin holds a sad record: it is believed to be the most heavily trafficked wild mammal in the world. The reason is human demand. In some places, pangolin scales and meat are wrongly believed to have value, and this belief has driven hunting to such a level that all eight species of pangolin are now threatened with extinction. Their gentle nature, which makes them roll into a ball rather than run, only makes them easier to catch. Saving the pangolin is difficult. They are shy, hard to study, and almost impossible to keep alive in captivity, so they cannot easily be bred in zoos. The most important work, conservationists agree, is to stop the demand that drives the hunting, through education and stronger laws. There is some hope. International trade in pangolins is now banned, and growing public awareness has begun to reduce demand in key markets. Yet the battle is far from won. The story of the pangolin is a reminder of a hard truth: an animal can be hunted to the edge of extinction for a value that exists only in human imagination, and saving it depends, above all, on changing human minds.

questions.prompt: What sad record does the pangolin hold?
options: The most heavily trafficked wild mammal in the world.

questions.prompt: Why are pangolins hunted?
options: Because their scales and meat are wrongly believed to have value.
Because they are dangerous to people.
For the loud sounds they make.

questions.prompt: Why is saving pangolins difficult?
options: They are shy and almost impossible to keep alive in captivity.
They are too fast to catch.
Nobody cares about them at all.

questions.prompt: What hard truth does their story show?
options: That an animal can be hunted for an imagined value, and saving it means changing minds.
That zoos are the complete answer.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 被走私最多的野生哺乳动物)、细节(B 鳞甲与肉被误以为有价值)、细节(B 害羞、难圈养繁育)、主旨(B 为想象中的价值被猎杀、拯救靠改变人心)。

sets.passage: In a growing number of cities around the world, cash is quietly disappearing. People pay for everything — from a morning coffee to a monthly rent — with a tap of a phone or a card. For many, this 'cashless society' is faster, cleaner and more convenient than carrying notes and coins. But the change brings hidden costs that are easy to overlook. The benefits are real. Digital payments are quick and can be safer, since there is less cash to be stolen. They also leave a record, which can make it harder to hide illegal activity. For businesses, handling less cash saves time and money. Yet not everyone gains. The elderly, the poor, and those without smartphones or bank accounts can find themselves shut out, unable to pay in shops that no longer accept cash. A society that abandons cash too quickly risks leaving its most vulnerable members behind. There are deeper worries too. Every digital payment creates a record of where we are and what we buy, raising serious questions about privacy. And in a fully cashless system, a power cut or computer failure could leave people suddenly unable to buy even food. The lesson is not that digital money is bad — it offers great advantages — but that the move away from cash should be gradual and thoughtful, making sure that convenience for the many does not become exclusion for the few.

questions.prompt: How do people pay in a cashless society?
options: With a tap of a phone or card.
By sending money in the post.

questions.prompt: Who can be shut out by a cashless society?
options: The elderly, the poor, and those without smartphones or bank accounts.

questions.prompt: What deeper worry does the passage raise?
options: Privacy, since every digital payment creates a record.

questions.prompt: What is the lesson of the passage?
options: The move away from cash should be gradual and thoughtful.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 手机或卡轻触支付)、细节(B 老人/穷人/无智能手机或账户者被排斥)、细节(B 隐私:每笔支付留痕)、主旨/推断(B 去现金应渐进而审慎)。

sets.passage: Maria had dreamed of being a dancer since she was a small child. She practised for years, and by sixteen she had won a place at a famous dance school. Then, just as her future seemed bright, a car accident left her with a serious injury to her leg. The doctors were gentle but clear: she would walk again, but she would never dance professionally. For a long time, Maria was lost in grief. Dance had been her whole identity, and without it she did not know who she was. But slowly, as she recovered, a new idea began to grow. If she could not perform, perhaps she could still share her love of dance in another way. She began to teach. At first she worked with small children, then, increasingly, with people whom traditional dance schools ignored: the elderly, the disabled, those who had been told they could not dance. She discovered a deep joy in helping others find in movement the freedom she had once felt herself. Maria never became the star she had dreamed of being. Yet today she runs a studio that has brought the joy of dance to hundreds of people who would otherwise never have known it. She often says that losing her dream of performing led her to a greater one. 'I thought my story was over,' she says, 'but it had only changed direction.'

questions.prompt: What ended Maria's dream of performing?
options: She lost all interest in dance.
A car accident injured her leg.

questions.prompt: What new idea did Maria have?
options: To share her love of dance by teaching others.
To take the driver to court.

questions.prompt: Who did Maria increasingly teach?
options: People ignored by dance schools, such as the elderly and disabled.

questions.prompt: What does Maria's story show?
options: That an injury must end all dreams.
That losing one dream can lead to a greater one.
That teaching is a kind of failure.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 车祸伤腿)、细节(B 以教学分享对舞蹈的热爱)、细节(B 教被舞校忽视的老人/残障者)、主旨/态度(B 失去一个梦想可通向更大的梦想)。

sets.passage: Around the world, roughly one third of all the food we produce is never eaten. At the same time, hundreds of millions of people go hungry. This strange and troubling contrast is one of the great wasteful failures of our age. Food is lost at every stage. On farms, crops rot before they can be harvested or sold. In transport and storage, more is spoiled. And in rich countries especially, huge amounts are simply thrown away by shops and households — food discarded for looking imperfect, or because a cautious 'best before' date has passed. The cost is more than moral. Wasted food means wasted water, land, energy and labour, all used to grow something that ends up in the bin. Rotting food also produces gases that contribute to climate change. If food waste were a country, scientists say, it would be among the world's largest producers of these gases. The encouraging news is that this is a problem we can all help to solve. Shops are learning to sell 'ugly' fruit and vegetables and to give unsold food to charities. At home, simple habits — planning meals, storing food properly, and using up leftovers — can cut waste sharply. Reducing food waste is one of the rare problems where doing the right thing also saves money. The solution lies not in some distant technology, but in the everyday choices of ordinary people.

questions.prompt: How much of the food we produce is never eaten?
Why is food thrown away in rich countries?
options: Because it looks imperfect or a 'best before' date has passed.
Because there is too little of it.

questions.prompt: Why is wasted food harmful beyond the moral cost?
options: It wastes water, land and energy and produces climate-changing gases.

questions.prompt: Where does the solution mainly lie?
options: In the everyday choices of ordinary people.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 约三分之一)、细节(B 因卖相差或过了保质期被丢)、细节(B 浪费水土能源、产生气候气体)、主旨(B 解决之道在普通人的日常选择)。

sets.passage: In a small mountain village, an old woman speaks a language that fewer than a hundred people in the world still understand. When she is gone, it may disappear forever. Her local dialect is one of thousands of small languages and dialects that are quietly vanishing across the globe. The causes are familiar. As young people move to cities for work and education, they adopt the dominant national language, which offers more opportunities. Television, the internet and schooling all push in the same direction. Within a generation or two, a dialect spoken for centuries can fall silent. Some argue that this is simply progress, that a shared common language helps people communicate and find work. There is truth in this. Yet something valuable is also lost. Each dialect carries its own way of seeing the world — its own words for local plants, its own songs, jokes and stories that cannot be fully translated. When a language dies, a unique piece of human culture dies with it. Around the world, people are now fighting to save endangered dialects, recording elderly speakers, creating dictionaries, and teaching the old words to children. Whether these efforts will succeed is uncertain. But they spring from a deep understanding: that the rich variety of human languages, like the variety of life in nature, is a treasure worth protecting before it is gone.

questions.prompt: Why are many small dialects vanishing?
options: Old people refuse to speak them.
Young people move to cities and adopt the dominant language.
There is no reason at all.

questions.prompt: What do some people argue about this change?
options: That dialects should be forced on everyone.
That it is progress, since a shared language helps communication.
That dialects are just useless noise.

questions.prompt: What is lost when a language dies?
options: A unique way of seeing the world and a piece of human culture.

questions.prompt: What understanding drives efforts to save dialects?
options: That all languages are really the same.
That the variety of human languages is a treasure worth protecting.
That dialects can never be saved.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 年轻人进城改用主导语言)、细节(B 有人称这是进步、通用语便于交流)、细节(B 一种独特的世界观与文化随之消亡)、主旨(B 语言的多样性是值得保护的财富)。

sets.passage: Every time a rocket is launched into space at enormous cost, some people ask a reasonable question: why spend so much money exploring distant worlds when there are so many problems to solve here on Earth? It is a fair challenge, and it deserves a serious answer. The most obvious reply is that space exploration brings practical benefits we now take for granted. Satellites, first developed for space programmes, give us weather forecasts, global communication, and the navigation systems in our phones. Countless everyday technologies, from medical devices to water filters, began as solutions to problems faced by astronauts. But the deeper value of space exploration is harder to measure. It pushes the limits of human knowledge and skill, inspiring generations of young people to study science. It answers, or tries to answer, some of the oldest questions humans have asked: How did the universe begin? Are we alone? Perhaps most importantly, looking outward changes how we see ourselves. The famous photographs of Earth taken from space — a small, fragile blue ball floating in darkness — gave humanity a powerful new sense of how precious and how shared our home really is. Space exploration, then, is not an escape from Earth's problems, but, in a strange way, a means of understanding them better. To explore the universe is also to look back, with new eyes, at ourselves.

questions.prompt: What question do some people ask about space exploration?
options: Why spend so much money in space when Earth has many problems.
Why the night sky is dark.

questions.prompt: What practical benefits came from space programmes?
options: Satellites, navigation systems and many everyday technologies.

questions.prompt: How did the photographs of Earth from space change us?
options: They gave a new sense of how precious and shared our home is.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's main point?
options: That space travel is a complete waste.
That exploring the universe also helps us understand ourselves and our home.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 地球有问题为何还花大钱探空)、细节(B 卫星/导航/众多日常技术)、细节(B 让人重新感知家园之珍贵与共有)、主旨(B 探索宇宙也是更好地理解自身)。

sets.passage: For years, the public library in our neighbourhood was a quiet, fading place. Its books were old, its building cold, and fewer and fewer people came. Many assumed that, in the age of the internet, the library had simply had its day. Then a new librarian arrived with a bold idea. She believed that a library should be not just a store of books, but a living centre of the community. With a small budget but great determination, she began to change things. She created a warm corner with comfortable chairs where people could read or simply rest. She started free classes — computer skills for the elderly, story hours for children, language lessons for newcomers. She turned an unused room into a space where local groups could meet. Slowly, people began to return. The library filled not only with readers but with learners, neighbours and friends. A lonely old man found company at the weekly chess club; a jobless young woman learned the computer skills that helped her find work; children who had never owned a book discovered the joy of reading. The library had been saved, but it had also been transformed. It proved that, far from being made useless by technology, libraries may be more valuable than ever — not only as places to find information, but as rare public spaces where, regardless of wealth, everyone is welcome.

questions.prompt: What was the library like before the new librarian arrived?
options: Quiet and fading, with few visitors.

questions.prompt: What did the new librarian believe?
options: That a library should be a living centre of the community.
That only the rich should come.

questions.prompt: What did she start in the library?
options: Free classes, reading corners and meeting spaces.

questions.prompt: What does the library's story prove?
options: That libraries may be more valuable than ever as welcoming public spaces.
That libraries should mainly sell books.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 冷清衰落、少有人来)、细节(B 图书馆应是社区的生活中心)、细节(B 办免费课程、阅读角、活动空间)、主旨(B 图书馆作为人人可入的公共空间比以往更可贵)。
